# Discussion

This systematic review synthesizes a decade of advances in the understanding, diagnosis, and treatment of hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) in adult populations, spanning 50 articles published between 2016 and 2026 across 36 journals. The body of evidence reveals a field undergoing rapid transformation, driven by four major advances that have fundamentally reshaped the clinical approach to this life-threatening syndrome.

## Synthesis of Major Advances

The first and arguably most consequential advance has been the validation of the interferon-gamma (IFN-gamma) axis as a central therapeutic target. The approval of emapalumab, an anti-IFN-gamma monoclonal antibody, for primary HLH represented a proof-of-concept milestone [@Locatelli2020Emapalumab]. This success validated immunological research identifying IFN-gamma as the pivotal cytokine driving macrophage activation in HLH [@Crayne2019The], and opened the door to pathway-specific strategies beyond etoposide-based protocols [@Bergsten2017Confirmed].

The second advance is the emergence of adult-specific management guidelines. The recommendations from La Rosee and colleagues on behalf of the Histiocyte Society marked a watershed moment [@Rose2019Recommendations]. These guidelines acknowledged that pediatric HLH-2004 diagnostic criteria are not validated for adults and that etoposide dose intensity from HLH-94 may cause unnecessary toxicity in older, comorbid patients. The guidelines advocated dose reductions, individualized treatment duration, and an age-dependent diagnostic approach.

Third, the etiological landscape of adult HLH has expanded dramatically with the recognition of iatrogenic triggers. Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy, now approved for multiple hematological malignancies, produces cytokine release syndrome (CRS) that can evolve into fulminant HLH in a subset of patients [@Neelapu2018Chimeric; @Brudno2016Toxicities; @Brudno2019Recent]. Treatment-related HLH deaths have been documented in trials of both axicabtagene ciloleucel and belantamab mafodotin [@Locke2019Longterm; @Lonial2020Belantamab], and a systematic meta-analysis has quantified the nonrelapse mortality burden of CAR T-cell therapy, with immune-mediated toxicity as a significant contributor [@Cordas2024A]. Similarly, immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) have been associated with HLH as a rare but severe immune-related adverse event [@RamosCasals2020Immunerelated], expanding the differential diagnosis that oncologists must consider when patients on immunotherapy develop unexplained cytopenias, hepatic dysfunction, and hyperferritinemia.

Fourth, the targeted therapy portfolio for hyperinflammatory syndromes has broadened considerably. Beyond emapalumab, the evidence now supports the use of tocilizumab for IL-6-driven CRS [@Kotch2019Tocilizumab; @ShimabukuroVornhagen2018Cytokine], anakinra for IL-1-mediated macrophage activation [@Shakoory2016Interleukin; @Dinarello2018Overview], and JAK inhibitors such as ruxolitinib for refractory hyperinflammation [@Jamilloux2019JAK]. This expanding armamentarium enables clinicians to match therapeutic interventions to the dominant cytokine pathway in individual patients, moving toward a precision medicine approach to what was previously treated as a monolithic syndrome.

## Evolution of the Field: From Pediatric Extrapolation to Adult-Specific Medicine

The trajectory of the adult HLH field between 2016 and 2026 reflects a critical transition from pediatric extrapolation to adult-specific approaches. Prior to this period, virtually all diagnostic criteria and treatment protocols were derived from the pediatric literature, most notably the HLH-2004 criteria and the HLH-94/HLH-2004 treatment protocols, which were designed for and validated in children with primary (genetic) HLH [@Bergsten2017Confirmed; @Ehl2018Recommendations]. The application of these frameworks to adults---who overwhelmingly present with secondary HLH triggered by infections, malignancies, or autoimmune disease---was always imperfect but was the only available option.

The revised classification of histiocytoses by Emile and colleagues in 2016, which formally positioned HLH and macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) within a unified nosological framework, provided an important conceptual foundation [@Emile2016Revised]. The 2016 MAS classification criteria for systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis by Ravelli and colleagues offered a disease-specific diagnostic tool for MAS in the rheumatological context [@Ravelli2016]. Together with the adult HLH recommendations from La Rosee and colleagues [@Rose2019Recommendations], these developments signaled a field-wide recognition that diagnostic and therapeutic approaches must be tailored to the specific disease context, patient age, and underlying trigger.

The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated this transition by exposing adult intensivists, pulmonologists, and infectious disease specialists to the HLH paradigm [@Mehta2020COVID; @Merad2020Pathological], permanently expanding the community of clinicians equipped to manage hyperinflammatory syndromes.

## Clinical Implications

Several practical implications emerge from this synthesis. First, clinicians should maintain a high index of suspicion for HLH in any critically ill adult with unexplained cytopenias, hepatic dysfunction, hyperferritinemia, and splenomegaly, particularly in the context of known triggers such as hematological malignancy, active infection, autoimmune flare, or recent immunotherapy. The HScore provides a validated probabilistic tool for screening and should be calculated whenever HLH is in the differential diagnosis [@Knaak2020Hemophagocytic].

Second, treatment urgency cannot be overemphasized. HLH is a medical emergency with mortality rates exceeding 50% in untreated adults. Early immunosuppressive therapy---whether with corticosteroids, etoposide, or targeted cytokine blockade---must be initiated on clinical suspicion, before definitive diagnostic confirmation, particularly in rapidly deteriorating patients. The adult guidelines explicitly endorse this approach, recognizing that waiting for all diagnostic criteria to be fulfilled may result in irreversible organ damage [@Rose2019Recommendations].

Third, the management of iatrogenic HLH requires nuanced decision-making. In the context of CAR T-cell therapy, distinguishing severe CRS from evolving HLH/MAS is critical because the therapeutic implications differ [@Neelapu2018Chimeric; @Teachey2016Identification]. While tocilizumab remains the first-line intervention for CRS, patients progressing to frank HLH may require corticosteroids, anakinra, or etoposide, and the decision to escalate therapy must balance the risk of abrogating antitumor efficacy against the risk of fatal hyperinflammation [@YakoubAgha2020Management; @Brudno2019Recent].

## Remaining Controversies

Despite substantial progress, several important controversies persist. The most fundamental is the absence of validated diagnostic criteria for adult HLH. The HLH-2004 criteria, still the most widely applied diagnostic framework, were developed for pediatric primary HLH and have never undergone formal validation in adult populations. Knaak and colleagues demonstrated that both HLH-2004 criteria and the HScore have significant diagnostic limitations in critically ill adults, where features such as cytopenias, hepatic dysfunction, and elevated ferritin are common in sepsis and other conditions, reducing specificity [@Knaak2020Hemophagocytic]. Developing adult-specific, prospectively validated diagnostic criteria remains the single most important unmet need in the field.

The optimal threshold for initiating etoposide in adults is another area of active debate. While etoposide is highly effective in HLH, its myelotoxicity poses particular risks in older adults with comorbidities and in patients with malignancy-associated HLH who may already have compromised bone marrow function. The adult guidelines suggest dose reduction, but the evidence supporting specific dose thresholds is limited [@Rose2019Recommendations; @Ehl2018Recommendations]. Whether targeted cytokine blockade can replace etoposide as first-line therapy in certain adult HLH subsets remains an open question.

The distinction between CRS and HLH in the context of CAR T-cell therapy continues to challenge clinicians. These entities share overlapping clinical features---fever, cytopenias, elevated ferritin and IL-6, hepatic dysfunction---and may represent a spectrum rather than discrete entities [@ShimabukuroVornhagen2018Cytokine; @Nastoupil2020Standardofcare]. The lack of consensus on where CRS ends and HLH begins has practical consequences for treatment timing, therapy selection, and prognostic communication.

## Strengths and Limitations

This review has several strengths. The systematic search across three major databases (Scopus, PubMed, and Embase) with PRISMA-compliant screening ensures comprehensive and reproducible literature retrieval. The CiteScore threshold of 3.0 or higher prioritizes evidence from established, peer-reviewed journals. The cross-disciplinary scope---encompassing hematology, immunology, rheumatology, oncology, and critical care---reflects the multidisciplinary nature of HLH and enables synthesis across traditionally siloed literatures.

Several limitations warrant acknowledgment. The CiteScore-based quality filter may have excluded valuable contributions from lower-impact specialty journals, particularly case series that inform the clinical characterization of rare disease subtypes. The absence of a formal meta-analysis limits the ability to generate pooled effect estimates for therapeutic interventions. The restriction to English-language publications may have excluded relevant regional evidence, particularly from East Asian and European centers with substantial HLH experience. Finally, the citation-based ranking approach may introduce a bias toward older publications and landmark studies, potentially underrepresenting recent but not yet highly cited contributions.

## Future Directions

The findings of this review point to several critical priorities for future research. First, the development and prospective validation of adult-specific diagnostic criteria for HLH should be a consensus priority. Such criteria must account for the higher baseline prevalence of cytopenias, hepatic dysfunction, and elevated inflammatory markers in critically ill adults, and should incorporate age-adjusted thresholds and novel biomarkers such as soluble CD163, soluble IL-2 receptor, and CXCL9 to improve specificity.

Second, biomarker-driven therapeutic selection represents the next frontier. The availability of targeted therapies against IFN-gamma, IL-1, IL-6, and the JAK-STAT pathway creates the possibility of matching patients to optimal therapy based on their individual cytokine profile rather than applying a uniform treatment protocol. Prospective trials evaluating biomarker-guided treatment algorithms are urgently needed.

Third, genetic profiling of adult HLH patients deserves systematic investigation. While primary HLH is predominantly a pediatric disease, hypomorphic variants in perforin pathway genes (PRF1, UNC13D, STX11, STXBP2) and newly identified susceptibility loci such as HAVCR2 (encoding TIM-3) [@Gayden2018Germline] may contribute to adult-onset disease, particularly in patients with recurrent episodes or refractory courses. Next-generation sequencing and whole-exome approaches [@Maffucci2016Genetic; @Heimall2017Immune] should be incorporated into the diagnostic evaluation of selected adult patients.

Fourth, combination targeted therapy approaches should be explored. Given that HLH involves simultaneous activation of multiple cytokine pathways, sequential or combination blockade---for example, anti-IFN-gamma with anti-IL-1 or JAK inhibition with IL-6 blockade---may prove more effective than monotherapy in refractory cases. Such combinations will require careful evaluation for additive immunosuppressive toxicity.

Fifth, optimizing the safety profile of CAR T-cell therapy with respect to HLH/MAS risk should be a priority. Identification of patient-level risk factors, refinement of CAR constructs to reduce off-target immune activation, and development of prophylactic strategies to prevent the transition from CRS to HLH will be essential as CAR T-cell indications expand to solid tumors and broader patient populations [@Schultz2022Disease; @Spiegel2021CAR; @Berdeja2021Ciltacabtagene].

# Conclusion

This systematic review of the adult HLH literature from 2016 to 2026 documents a field in the midst of a fundamental transformation. The decade has witnessed the validation of targeted cytokine blockade as a viable therapeutic paradigm, exemplified by the success of emapalumab against IFN-gamma and the repurposing of tocilizumab, anakinra, and ruxolitinib across multiple hyperinflammatory contexts. The first adult-specific management guidelines have been published, signaling a necessary departure from the uncritical application of pediatric protocols to adult patients. The etiological spectrum of HLH has expanded to include iatrogenic triggers---CAR T-cell therapy and immune checkpoint inhibitors---that are increasingly prevalent as cancer immunotherapy becomes standard of care. The COVID-19 pandemic served as both a stress test and an accelerator, exposing the clinical overlap between viral hyperinflammation and HLH while simultaneously highlighting critical immunological differences and expanding the community of clinicians familiar with HLH diagnostic and management principles.

Yet significant challenges remain. Adult diagnostic criteria are unvalidated, the optimal role of etoposide versus targeted therapy in adults is undefined, and the boundary between CRS and HLH remains blurred. The path forward requires prospective, multicenter trials to validate diagnostic algorithms, biomarker-guided treatment strategies, and systematic genetic profiling to identify adults at risk. As the therapeutic armamentarium expands and the population exposed to potential HLH triggers grows, the imperative to improve early recognition and individualized management has never been greater. Reducing the unacceptably high mortality of adult HLH through evidence-based, precision-oriented care is now within reach, but will require sustained collaboration across hematology, immunology, rheumatology, oncology, and critical care.
